input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Lab‑scale gantry crane comprising a mechanical gantry frame, an inertially controlled vertical screw drive, a horizontal carriage with payload stages, and an associated electronic controller; operated within a controlled laboratory environment for research and educational demonstrations. Its key characteristics that make it suitable for digital twinning include a well‑defined kinematic chain, straightforward sensor integration for real‑time state feedback, and a small, repeatable footprint that facilitates accurate CAD modeling and simulation of its motion dynamics.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin exposes a suite of services: (1) Optimal motion‑control synthesis for the crane, providing real‑time trajectory planning and speed modulation; (2) Historical data logging that records state trajectories, control inputs, and sensor outputs for offline analysis; (3) Data visualization dashboards that render live and archived kinematic and operational metrics; and (4) Continuous validation workflows that compare real‑time measurements with the kinematic model to detect deviations and faults.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: GreenhouseDT orchestrates its digital twin through an event‑driven architecture, where sensor readings and twin model updates are passed via an ActiveMQ JMS broker and persisted in an InfluxDB time‑series database. Each component—sensor interface, model logic, adaptation engine—is packaged in a separate virtual machine image, simplifying deployment and isolation. The Air Quality Management exemplar uses a Raspberry‑Pi host for physical sensors, models the plant via AutomationML for formal description, and pipelines data through Microsoft Azure services (IoT Hub, Azure Functions) for runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The lab‑scale gantry crane adopts a similar ad‑hoc composition; its control logic and twin representations are containerised, and inter‑service communication is mediated by message queues (ActiveMQ in GreenhouseDT; Azure Event Grid in Air‑Quality). No centralized twin orchestration framework is employed, and coordination between instances relies on the broker and explicit messaging contracts.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is performed through standard protocols and cloud services. In GreenhouseDT, sensor firmware on Raspberry‑Pi devices publishes data to ActiveMQ, which forwards it to InfluxDB; external stakeholders can subscribe to the broker or query the time‑series database. The Air Quality Management prototype exposes its twin via a Microsoft Azure IoT Hub, delivering telemetry to Azure Functions and Azure Machine Learning for real‑time prediction, thereby enabling integration with other Azure‑based digital twins or enterprise systems. The gantry crane case, while not explicitly detailed, follows a containerised approach that can expose RESTful APIs or message queues to upstream logistics or SCADA systems, enabling interoperability via HTTP/REST and JMS. The exemplars rely on standard open‑source tools rather than proprietary integration layers, ensuring interoperability across heterogeneous environments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The GreenhouseDT exemplar employs an InfluxDB time‑series database to persist greenhouse sensor data and actuation logs, with incoming measurements and control commands streamed via an Apache ActiveMQ message broker. The digital twin synchronizes a behavioral runtime model with the physical greenhouse plant, enabling adaptive control of environmental parameters. Pre‑recorded greenhouse data sets are loaded into the virtual machine images upon deployment, providing a basis for simulation and validation of the behavioral model.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from the physical gantry crane is collected by the Logging Application, a Python program that continuously polls the crane controllers via the Physical Crane Interface. The retrieved sensor readings (e.g., position, speed, acceleration, and load) are written to a TimescaleDB time‑series database over a TCP‑based PostgreSQL connection using the Python psycopg2 driver. The Logging Application batches records every second, timestamps each entry, and applies basic sanitation checks before insertion. The recorded telemetry is subsequently published to the local MQTT broker, making it available to other DT services such as the Continuous Validation Service and the Dashboard.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are issued by the Control Application, which uses the Physical Crane Interface to send movement instructions directly to the crane controllers. Commands are represented as plain Python dictionaries containing target joint positions, velocities, and trajectory parameters, serialized as JSON and transmitted over the same TCP interface used for sensing. The physical hardware acknowledges each command via status flags returned in the next sensor poll. While no explicit authentication or access control mechanisms are detailed, the Control Application operates locally on the operator’s workstation. Conflict resolution is delegated to the crane’s built‑in safety interlocks.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT services communicate through a local MQTT broker (e.g., Mosquitto) running over TCP for publish/subscribe messaging between services such as the Trajectory Generator, Simulation Service, and Continuous Validation Service. Database interactions use the PostgreSQL protocol over TCP to a TimescaleDB instance, while Grafana dashboards connect over HTTP/HTTPS to visualize data queried from TimescaleDB. The network topology is a flat local‑host or LAN configuration with low latency (<1 ms). Reliability is supported by MQTT persistent sessions and TimescaleDB’s inherent replication features, though explicit redundancy mechanisms are not specified.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: GreenhouseDT is developed as a self‑adaptive exemplar on a low‑cost greenhouse, employing virtual machine images pre‑loaded with recorded greenhouse sensor data. The twin architecture uses an InfluxDB time‑series database for telemetry ingestion and an ActiveMQ message broker to publish and subscribe to real‑time events. The source code and VM images are hosted on GitHub, providing explicit version control via Git. Air‑Quality Management uses a Raspberry‑Pi equipped with environmental sensors; its digital twin is described using AutomationML and is integrated into a Microsoft Azure pipeline that orchestrates data ingestion, simulation, and prediction tasks. Both exemplars rely on containerisation or virtualization to enable rapid deployment, and versioning of the twin models and runtime artifacts is managed through GitHub repositories. The documents do not detail a formal twin‑engineering methodology or QA testing procedures.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Air‑Quality Management exemplar exposes three use‑cases: runtime visualisation, physics‑based or data‑driven simulation, and predictive analytics to aid virus‑spread prevention. In practice, telemetry from the Raspberry‑Pi is streamed via Azure IoT Hub to an Azure Stream Analytics job that aggregates temperature, humidity, CO₂, and particulate‑matter values, generating real‑time dashboards. Simulations are run on Azure Batch using a pre‑defined scenario set, producing forecasted concentration fields that support operators in making ventilation‑adjustment decisions. Decisions are mediated through an interface that associates each insight with an estimated confidence level derived from statistical error bounds or sensor‑quality metadata. No explicit ranking or automated recommendation engine is documented.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The digital‑twin modelling layer in the Air‑Quality Management exemplar is expressed in AutomationML, an ISO‑based interchange format for plant and automation systems (ISO 11782). The exemplar also relies on Microsoft Azure’s IoT Hub and Stream Analytics services, which adhere to Azure’s reference architecture for the Internet of Things and comply with Azure Industrial IoT security guidelines. No other industry certification or interoperability framework is mentioned.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)



















Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
